sections.paragraph:
-

(1) to flow, to drop; whence מַר a drop, מֹר myrrh, so called from distilling, and prob. מְרֵרָה bile. (Arab. مَرْمَرَ to cause to flow, مَرْمَرَةُ frequent rain, and مَمَرُّ channel. The trilit. مَرَّ has often the sense of going, passing away, which in many roots is connected with that of flowing; see הָלַךְ No. 4, جرى to run, to flow; Aram. רְהַט to run; whence רְהָטִים channels.)

(2) to be bitter. (Arab. مَرَّ fut. A. id., and so in all the cognate languages; also the Lat. amarus, also mœreo. How this notion coheres with the former is not clear. Perhaps it is denom, from מֹר myrrh, and מְרֵרָה bile, as being very bitter things). Impers. מַר לִי it is bitter to me, i.e. I am sad, Lam 1:4, followed by מִן (because of any thing), Rth 1:13. Fut. A. יֵמַר Isa 24:9, compare Hebr. Gram. § 67, note 3.

(3) to be embittered, 1Sa 30:6.

Piel, fut. יְמָרֵר.-

(1) to make any thing bitter, Exo 1:14 Isa 22:4, אֲמָרֵר בַּבֶּבִי “I weep bitterly.”

(2) to embitter, to irritate, to provoke any one, compare Hithp. Gen 49:23.

Hiphil הֵמַר, inf. הָמֵר.-

(1) to make life bitter, Job 27:2.

(2) followed by לְ, to make any one sad, Rth 1:20, הֶמַר לִי שַׁדַּי “the Almighty hath made me sad.” Compare הֵרַע לְּ verse 21 .

(3) to weep bitterly, for the fuller הֵמַר בְּכִי Zec 12:10.

Note. תַּמֵּר Exo 23:21, is fut. Hiphil in the Chald. form, for תָּמֵר, but with the signification taken from the verb מָרָה, followed by בְּ to rebel. Compare the note under מָרָה.

Hithpalpel הִתְמַרְמַר to be embittered exasperated, Dan 8:7. (Syr. ܡܰܪܡܰܪ to embitter, to provoke; Arab. مَرْمَرَ to be angry.)

Derivatives, מַר, מָרָה [מָרָּה], מְרִירִי, מְרִירוּת, ממֶר, מַמְּרֹרִים, תַּמְרוּרִים, pr.n. מָרָא [מָרֹות], and those which immediately follow.
